trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline established at 3.25ms with score 50-60. Profiling complete: GEMM 88.7%, transpose 9.1%, elementwise 2.1%. Multiple failed approaches documented. Advancing to optimization stage.

nudges.text: Writing a competitive Triton conv2d kernel from scratch is extremely difficult — even if you fix the correctness bug (likely a grid dimension limit; try using a 2D/3D grid with `tl.program_id(1)` for batch), your kernel is 120x slower. Consider a faster path: first try `torch.compile` on the existing `nn.Conv2d`, or try switching to NHWC memory format (`x.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)`) which avoids the transpose overhead you saw in profiling (9.2%). You can always attempt a custom Triton kernel later if those don't beat baseline.

Your custom Triton kernel is ~120x slower than PyTorch's MIOpen conv, and the benchmark timed out. Writing a fast conv2d in Triton from scratch is not viable here. Please pivot immediately: create a `ModelNew` that simply uses `nn.Conv2d` but with NHWC layout (`x = x.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)`, weight in channels_last too) to eliminate the ~9% transpose overhead you profiled, or try wrapping the forward pass with `torch.compile`. Either approach should give you a passing score quickly and you can iterate from there.

Your channels_last attempt was slower because you're converting the input in `forward()`. Try this: at `__init__`, call `self.conv = self.conv.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)`, and in `forward`, convert input once with `x = x.contiguous(memory_format=torch.channels_last)` then call `self.conv(x)`. Also set `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True` at module init. The 9% transpose overhead from profiling should disappear if both weights and input are already NHWC before the conv kernel launches.
